text: I usually have some toast with butter and jam, maybe a cup of tea, and perhaps some scrambled eggs or baked beans if I'm feeling more indulgent. If my grandkids come over, we might make pancakes together, which is always fun! A simple, comforting start to the day, really.

I usually have a simple meal while watching the news on telly or catching up on some reading before my usual mid-afternoon dip. Sometimes I'll make something like sandwiches, leftovers, or soup, other times I might heat up a frozen meal or have a bowl of cereal. If I'm feeling more energetic, I might even bake something for myself later in the afternoon, perhaps some biscuits or a cake for grandkids who visit. It's all quite informal really, nothing too fancy!
I tend to be a bit of an early bird, so I'm usually in the sack by 10 pm. It helps me feel rested for the morning, which is my most productive part of the day.

I think a bit of self-care is essential, even at my age! For me, it starts the moment I put my feet on the ground after waking up. A cuppa tea, maybe some gentle stretching, and I'm ready to tackle the day. It's all about setting a gentle pace and being mindful of how I feel. If I need a little extra rest during the afternoon slump – which usually happens between two o'clock and four – I'll take a short walk outside or sit quietly with a nice cuppa while catching up on some reading. In the evenings, before bed, I like to unwind with a warm bath, perhaps watching something soothing on telly, or enjoying a quiet chat with my grandkids via FaceTime. Nothing too fancy, really – just taking pleasure in the simple moments and making sure I get enough sleep so I can be happy and energised for the next day.

I think networking is essential for staying informed about what's going on within the organisation, even though I'm fully retired now. Before retirement, my job was always keeping me busy enough! In those days, we'd have regular meetings where everyone could catch up and share any relevant information. Now that I'm out, I still make an effort to reach out to some colleagues who remain in the field to see how things are progressing. We'll meet for a cuppa every so often to discuss current projects, share tips, and swap advice – all informal, really. Nothing too formal, just friendly chats. It helps feel like I'm still part of the team, even if I am officially retired.

I find a regular morning routine helps me stay focused. I like to have some sort of plan for the day, even though my work is flexible now since retiring. A nice cuppa, getting dressed, and sitting comfortably while checking emails and responding to any urgent messages sets me up nicely before diving into whatever task requires my attention. Sometimes I'll listen to calming music or nature sounds to block out distractions – I used to love gardening, so I still try to incorporate some soothing atmosphere whenever possible! Once I'm settled in, I tend to be quite productive until my mid-afternoon slump hits, usually around two o'clock. After that, I take a break and rest my eyes, perhaps read something enjoyable or chat with someone. Then, I finish up what remains of the day feeling refreshed.

I have a grandchild who lives far away, so we use the internet for regular video calls whenever he's free. He helps me out with some technical stuff like setting up the equipment and getting everything sorted, which is lovely. It makes us feel more close despite the distance. When using them myself, I find it quite easy once I'm set up – I'll usually sit comfortably, put my reading glasses on, and chat away. A bit fiddly sometimes though; always seems to need adjusting something! My grandson does say I look silly wearing these funny contraptions on strings round my neck...

I must say, getting used to the freedom is quite a process! At first, I found myself missing the routine and purpose that came with having an office job. But my grandkids have been such a great support system – we set little goals together, like tending to our garden or completing projects, which helps me feel productive. And, of course, staying busy with hobbies like reading and gardening really takes up most of my free time. Plus, knowing that there's always something to look forward to, whether it's a visit from one of them or a fun activity planned, gives me motivation. I think it's all about finding what works for me and making sure I'm keeping myself engaged and challenged.

To be honest, I never really had too much trouble adjusting to changes during my working years. As a mid-level admin role, there were always some adjustments needed due to policy shifts, staffing changes, or department reorganisations. But overall, we managed quite smoothly. What helped was having a steady handover process, knowing who was taking charge, and making sure everyone understood the reasons behind the change. If something significant came along, our team usually rallied together and adapted rather quickly. Of course, sometimes it took getting used to, but we all worked towards finding solutions. Now that I'm fully retired, I find myself being more flexible still – perhaps because life has taught me that flexibility is key!

I think I'm quite straightforward, actually. If there's something bothering me or a concern regarding my workload or responsibilities, I'll speak clearly and directly to the relevant people involved. As an experienced admin assistant, I know how to communicate effectively and professionally, even when discussing sensitive topics like staffing issues or difficult clients. My years in education have taught me the importance of being articulate and assertive while still showing respect and consideration for others.

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a deep breath, step back, and remind myself that people often jump to conclusions or misinterpret what we say because we're all limited in our perspective. I think to myself, "Is there more to the story?" rather than getting defensive straight away. Then I usually ask clarifying questions to make sure everyone involved is on the same page. It's amazing how much confusion can be cleared up once you listen actively and give someone space to share their thoughts. If necessary, I'll rephrase my original message to avoid any misunderstandings going forward.

I love being surrounded by my loved ones! When we're together, I usually try to take charge a bit and make sure everyone has enough food and drinks. You know how much I adore cooking and baking, so I always end up making something special for the occasion. My grandkids just adore my scones and cakes, and I have to admit, it brings me joy seeing them happy. During meals, we tend to chat about our lives – what's been going on, any funny moments, or exciting plans. The kids are all growing up so fast now; it feels like just yesterday I was playing games with them instead of having conversations with grown-ups. We laugh a lot, too – those little rascals always manage to find some way to make us giggle! After dinner, sometimes we'll play board games or watch a movie together. It's lovely watching them interact with each other; they're such different personalities, but they complement each other perfectly. And, of course, there's plenty of arguing – siblings will argue, but it's just part of their growth process, isn't it? Sometimes, though, we might have more subdued evenings where we talk about serious topics, sharing stories or reminiscing about old memories. Those quieter moments mean just

To be honest, I'm a bit wary of these modern networking platforms, even though my son says they're essential for keeping in touch with people professionally. To me, there's too much emphasis on presenting an idealised version of yourself and I find all the fuss rather overwhelming. If someone wants to connect with me, they know how to reach me via phone or post. My granddaughter helps me set up simple posts every now and again, like sharing our grandkids' birthdays and whatnot, so that feels more manageable.

I think sensitivity is key when approaching delicate subjects with people. You have to be genuine and considerate, making sure the other person feels heard and understood. As a former admin worker, I've had my fair share of dealing with difficult conversations – whether it was addressing concerns with patients, discussing challenging issues with colleagues, or even navigating tricky family dynamics. My rule of thumb has always been to remain level-headed, listen attentively to what the other person is saying, and respond thoughtfully. It's essential to tread carefully, avoiding being judgmental or confrontational. Sometimes, taking a step back and giving someone space to express themselves can really make all the difference. Of course, there will still be disagreements, but having an open mind and being respectful helps to diffuse tension and find common ground.

I'd say I'm fairly straightforward with people. If someone tries to persuade me to do something I'm not keen on, I'll usually tell them my mind and explain why I don't think it's a good idea. I value being honest and transparent, even if it might make others uncomfortable. Of course, there have been instances where I've had to compromise and found common ground, but ultimately, I try to stick to what feels right for myself. My husband used to always say that I was too straight-forward, but I suppose he was probably right! Now, since his passing, I find I rely more on trusting my instincts rather than going along with the crowd.

I'm a bit wary of those targeted adverts, to be honest. They seem like an invasion of my personal space. When I shop online, I try to avoid clicking on them whenever possible, and I always make sure to close the tab once I've finished browsing. My grandsons sometimes tease me about being too paranoid, but I'd rather play it safe!

I find myself relying heavily on the 'pin tab' feature most of the time - it helps me come back to where I was without having to remember every single website. And I use a relatively simple layout for my bookmarks too, so everything is quite straightforward. Occasionally I ask one of the grandkids to show me how something works, though! They're always happy to lend a hand.

I try to ignore them, really. If someone is being deliberately hurtful or abusive, there's no point engaging. But most people who make those sorts of remarks usually say something more out of frustration themselves. You know, a bit like how my late husband used to grumble about the weather – sometimes he'd lash out because it was miserable outside! Anyway, I find that rising above what others think and focusing on my own work helps me avoid getting drawn into their negativity. Besides, there's so much lovely content available online these days; it's easy enough to steer myself towards the nice bits!

I'm always a bit flustered when the internet connection goes out or my audio isn't coming through properly! My grandsons usually step in to sort me out, bless them. They're very tech-savvy and know how to troubleshoot. Sometimes we have to try different browsers or find an alternative way to connect. It can be quite frustrating, but thankfully, they're always there to lend a hand.

I make sure to have a separate folder for each bill, labelled clearly so I know what's inside, and then use my calendar to mark the due dates. My grandsons are always happy to remind me on birthdays or special occasions. If one is away from home, another will give them a call. It helps having two sets of eyes on keeping track! I try to pay most everything electronically whenever possible, which makes it easier to set reminders and avoid missing payments. And I'm very particular about making cheques go out before the deadline too – nothing like sending something off in advance to be safe!

I'm a bit fussy when it comes to cancelling subscriptions. I think before I make the cut. You know, what am I really using regularly enough to justify keeping it? If it's something I haven't opened an app for months, like some streaming service, I'll probably give it another month or two before deciding whether to stick with it or bin it. But if I find myself re-subscribing every quarter because I forgot how to use one thing or another... well, that's usually my cue to reassess and consider letting go. I suppose it sounds silly, but I hate feeling wasteful!

I'm a saver, always have been, so we think twice before making big ticket purchases these days. We like to consider whether it's absolutely necessary and make sure there'll be enough left over afterwards for our grandkids, in case something comes along where they might need some money in the future. Before buying anything significant, my late husband used to say "waste not, want not" – still live by those words!

I'm a saver, so I always try to stick with what works best value-wise. If something is still in decent condition and serves its purpose, there's little reason to splurge on brand-new. Used items have already taken the biggest hit in price, so I think I can usually find a better deal. Of course, some people might say "well, it'll last longer," but I like to be practical – I believe that a bit of TLC will make any item work out fine, even an older model. Plus, reducing waste feels right; every penny counts!

I think the "waste not, want not" attitude has stuck with me since childhood. My mum always said we should make every penny count, so I try to be very mindful of my spending habits. When I'm out shopping, I stick to what I need rather than buying something just because it's on sale. And I never impulse buy – those moments usually pass before I even know what I've bought! Also, whenever there have been opportunities to save money in the past, like during our big shop sales periods, I've tried to take advantage of them. If someone else is going to pay for a service, such as getting my hair done, I'll offer to pick them up instead. It might seem small, but these little acts add up! At work, being an efficient admin assistant was key; keeping track of receipts, making sure orders were processed correctly... all those things helped build my sense of discipline and taught me how much more valuable each pound could be. Even now, I regularly check bank statements and adjust budgets accordingly.

I try to take advantage of them whenever I can! My late husband used to say I was a "saleswoman" even before he met me. He'd tease me because I love snagging deals and stocking up on non-perishables during the holidays. Now, since his passing, my children still know how much I like a bargain, so they'll call me occasionally to warn me about upcoming sales and remind me to make a list. I might treat myself to something nice every now and then too – perhaps a cookbook or some gardening tools. But overall, I'm quite frugal, always looking out for opportunities to save a bit extra for those grandkids who come to visit.

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned eater, I suppose. My mum always said, "Waste not, want not," so we never really had much food going to waste in our household growing up. As a result, I think I've developed a habit of eating smaller portions over the years. It's not that I find myself counting calories or worrying about being strict – it's more that I just know what feels like a decent size serving is probably sufficient for me. Still, I'll admit that if there's something left over, I might indulge every now and then!

I think a gentle, non-judgmental approach is best. I'd like someone who listens attentively and helps me work out my thoughts and feelings rather than telling me what to do. A bit like having a trusted friend, really. Someone who understands the human side of things, not just the facts and figures. And preferably face-to-face, too – there's something about being able to read body language and facial expressions that gets lost in writing.

Well, I think the key is finding a balance between being busy and taking breaks. As someone who loves getting things done and keeping busy, I have to be mindful of my limits. A nice cuppa every now and then helps too! Seriously though, I make sure to pace myself during work periods – like setting aside shorter sessions or stepping away from a task altogether when needed. Taking a walk outside can really refresh me, even if only for 15 minutes. And of course, having something lovely to look forward to later makes all the difference – whether it's spending time with grandkids, tending to my garden, or enjoying some quiet time with a good book. It's funny how a bit of rest actually gets more done afterwards, isn't it?!

I think the key is establishing a consistent bedtime routine, which helps signal to my brain that it's time to wind down. So, every night, I read something before bed – usually an engaging novel or some history. It really relaxes me and clears my mind. Also, making sure my room is dark, cool, and quiet is essential; blackout curtains have been a lifesaver! A warm cup of herbal tea might be helpful too – chamomile does seem to work better than peppermint for me. And of course, getting ready for bed slightly earlier each night has helped regulate my body clock.

I try to mix it up a bit, even though my body isn't what it used to be! Most weekdays, I stick to gentle brisk walks along the local park paths or round our quiet streets. On Saturdays, I like to tend to me garden – digging, weeding, planting – gets some fresh air and exercise while enjoying nature. Occasionally, I'll join forces with me granddaughter and her mother for a bike ride into the nearby woods. We all cycle together, chatting away, which makes it feel more like fun rather than exercise! On Sundays, I usually take a leisurely stroll to the local market, browsing stalls, buying some fresh produce...and maybe stopping for a scone or two. It's nice to have something to look forward to every Sunday morning.

I tend to think step-by-step, weighing the pros and cons very seriously before making a decision. It's almost like creating an inventory of all the advantages and disadvantages of each choice, which helps me narrow down my options. Sometimes, having some guidance or advice from trusted people in relevant fields will make sense too – like talking to someone who's done something similar to what I'm considering. Ultimately, common sense plays a big part in how I decide; sometimes you have to rely on your instincts, knowing that there isn't always one definitive way of solving a particular issue.

I think the key is being open to constructive criticism and taking a step back to consider alternative viewpoints before deciding how to proceed. It helps me put my mind on the task at hand and try different approaches until we find one that works best for everyone involved. When someone points out something isn't quite right, I like to ask them what specifically didn't work so I can better understand their perspective and make adjustments accordingly. Sometimes, it takes a bit of reflection and consideration, but ultimately, incorporating feedback makes everything run more smoothly and efficiently in the end.

I think my approach is quite straightforward really. When faced with a problem or puzzle, I like to take a step back, gather all the facts and information available, make some notes, and then start piecing together what makes sense. It's almost like putting together a jigsaw puzzle – you need to find the right pieces first! Sometimes, breaking things down into smaller parts helps me tackle them one by one. And of course, being methodical and thorough is key; I hate leaving stones unturned. If needed, I'll ask someone else for an outside perspective too – fresh eyes can be very helpful. But ultimately, I believe common sense and logical reasoning should always guide the way.

I find writing things down helps me a lot. When I'm unsure about something, I'll jot some notes on a piece of paper or type them into my iPad. Sometimes having everything in one place makes sense of the confusion and allows me to think more clearly. My grandsons have even helped me set up a digital note-taking system; now I use it regularly! It's amazing how much clearer things become once you put pen to paper... or fingers to keyboard.

I think one way I'd like to improve is being more tech-savvy. As much as I value the benefits of staying connected with my loved ones through digital means, I find all these changes so... overwhelming! It feels like everything has changed overnight, and I'm still getting used to navigating a world where half the population seems to be glued to their devices. If I could, I'd love to take some courses or work with someone who could teach me how to use those newer gadgets properly – maybe even set them up myself, bit by bit. Getting stuck on something always stresses me out; knowing there's an easier way makes me feel frustrated because I should know better! Another area I'd focus on improving is perhaps expanding my horizons, taking risks outside of what's comfortable. Sometimes my tendency towards caution holds me back from exploring opportunities. What might seem daunting now will become exciting once experienced, and stepping into uncertainty helps build confidence. Maybe starting smaller steps, joining a local community group, attending workshops, or meeting people from different backgrounds will allow me to discover hidden talents or simply broaden my perspective.

Well, I think the key is taking a step-by-step approach. You know, being prepared, having some idea what might happen, but still allowing yourself space to adjust. It's all about finding that balance between confidence and caution. For me, starting small helps – whether it's trying something new like cooking a recipe or joining an activity – gives you a chance to build momentum and feel more secure before diving deeper. And it's essential to be kind to oneself along the way; remember, it's okay to make mistakes! A bit of humility goes a long way. With patience and persistence, one discovers that even seemingly daunting tasks become manageable. As I always say, "patience and curiosity" have gotten me far throughout my life.

I always make sure to carve out a bit of me time each morning before the rest of the world wakes up. A cuppa, some quiet time, and getting lost in a good book is just what I need to start the day feeling refreshed and focused. It's usually while my husband was still awake, actually – we'd sit together, have our tea, and he'd listen intently to whatever nonsense I got myself into. Now that he's gone...well, it's more one-on-one between us, really. My grandkids might join me later, but first thing in the mornin', it's just me and the pages.

I'm a bit of an avid reader, even though my energy levels might suggest otherwise! *laughs* You know, people often say that reading is the perfect way to relax, and I think there's some truth to that. When I'm feeling fresh and alert in the morning, I love browsing through libraries or second-hand bookshops looking for something new. Sometimes I'll pick up books based on recommendations from friends, like our book club meetings – we always have such lively discussions afterwards! Also, I must admit that I still enjoy watching nature documentaries; sometimes those programmes spark interesting topics or themes that make me wonder what I'd like to read more about. And occasionally, my grandkids will come across fascinating titles while playing games on their tablets, which piques my interest too. But generally speaking, I rely quite heavily on word-of-mouth suggestions, usually from trusted sources like librarians, fellow readers, or the radio station where they discuss literature – Radio 4 has an excellent programme called "Start the Week" that really gets me into various subjects. Once I start reading one thing, I tend to get hooked on the author or genre, so I try to branch out gradually, exploring different styles and periods...

I like to knit. It helps me relax and unwind, especially during the morning when I'm sipping my tea before a busy day ahead. There's something soothing about creating patterns with yarn that clears my mind and gets my creative juices flowing. I find myself lost in thought, watching the stitches come together, and before I know it, an hour has slipped away!

I find that while I'm interested in listening to them, I still prefer a bit of visual stimulation like having the words in front of me, so I usually stick to audio books only during my daily commute or while doing some chores. Occasionally, if I know someone has recommended something really interesting, I might give podcasting a go, though I have to admit, I sometimes struggle to make sense of all those different formats!

I love staying active! You know, people always think retirement is all leisurely strolls and napping, but I'm still very much the energetic sort. My favourite thing to do is tend to my garden – there's something so satisfying about watching those little seedlings grow into beautiful flowers and delicious vegetables. It's a great way to spend an afternoon, getting some exercise while being outdoors and enjoying nature. When the weather permits, I'll put on my wellies and take a walk along the nearby canal path or visit our local park. The fresh air does me a world of good, and I like keeping fit to make sure I can keep up with the grandkids when we go out together. Occasionally, I might join a friend for a game of bridge or bowling; these sorts of things keep my mind sharp and provide a bit of friendly competition. Occasionally, I'll treat myself to a cooking class or workshop where I can try something new, like making chocolates or preserving fruit. And, of course, I adore attending local history lectures and talks – such fascinating stuff! But I have to say, my absolute passion remains tending to my garden. There's nothing quite like getting your hands dirty and seeing what blooms come out the other end!

I find solitude quite lovely. It gives me a chance to recharge my batteries and focus on myself, whether that's reading a good book, tending to my garden, or simply enjoying a cuppa in peace. As an ambivert, I'm happy with a mix of social interactions and some quieter moments to myself. When I'm feeling a bit drained, being alone helps me unwind and come back refreshed for the people who matter most in my life. Plus, having spent many years in busy admin jobs, there's something therapeutic about having time to myself where I can let my mind wander!

When trying a new cuisine, I tend to stick to dishes that look fairly familiar, like vegetable-based curries or pasta sauces, really. If someone recommends something completely foreign to me, such as Korean BBQ or Ethiopian injera bread, I might politely decline unless there's an element of the dish that sounds interesting. For instance, if my granddaughter was going on holiday to Japan, I'd be fascinated to try some authentic Japanese food while we're out together – maybe sushi or ramen noodles. But ordering full-blown unfamiliar meals is usually outside my comfort zone. Still, being introduced to different flavours has broadened my palate slightly!

I find that mixing between quieter moments and more engaging activities helps me feel like I'm getting the most out of my free time. For instance, I love spending a morning tending to my vegetable patch – there's something therapeutic about watching those seeds sprout into actual veggies! After that, maybe we'll have a nice cuppa and some chat with our local neighbour before heading off to the library to catch up on some historical novels. Occasionally, we might pop into town for a spot of browsing at the market stall selling handmade crafts; it's lovely to pick up inspiration for future projects. On other occasions, my grandkids will come over, and we'll spend an afternoon playing games or having a bake together. It really is wonderful seeing them develop their creative side! As for digital diversions, I must admit I struggle keeping up, what with all these newer gadgets coming along, but I still appreciate being able to look up a recipe on my trusty old iPad – my grandson was kind enough to set me up with one, bless him! Sometimes, though, even simple pleasures count, such as sitting quietly in my armchair with a good book and enjoying a warm cup of tea – perfect way to unwind after a busy day.

I like keeping a little notebook where I jot down my plans and progress, mainly for the grandkids so they know what we're going to do together. It helps me remember too! Sometimes I'll make a list of the books I'm currently reading, along with some notes about them. And occasionally, I'll have an idea for something I'd like to try in the garden, so I note those down there as well. Nothing fancy, just a simple A5 booklet and pens – easy enough for anyone to use, even Joan, who's not exactly tech-savvy.

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned stickler for routines and structures, so I have to admit I rely quite heavily on my trusty planners and calendars! My diary is always filled with notes and reminders, and I colour-code everything – red for deadlines, blue for appointments, green for birthdays... You know the drill! At work, I used to be very meticulous about labeling files and folders, keeping all documents in neat stacks, and using those little sticky tabs to keep track of different projects. Now that I'm retired, I still like to tidy up each morning before starting my daily tasks, making sure I've got all my essentials laid out ready to go. For me, having some sort of system helps prevent chaos taking hold, even now that I only have one grandchild living nearby most of the time. It might seem old-fashioned, but I find being prepared really reduces stress and makes my life run more smoothly!

Before leaving the house, I usually have a cuppa and check my schedule for any upcoming meetings or appointments, make sure I'm prepared with all necessary documents and equipment. Then, I'll put on some comfortable shoes, grab my handbag, and head out into the morning sunlight – whenever that is! The bus will be waiting outside, so I might take that if I need to travel far away, otherwise, I'll walk along our quiet streets until I reach my destination. Sometimes I stop by the local shop to pick up a few essentials like milk or bread on my way out.

Well, I think the key is making a conscious effort to turn lights out whenever not needed, using curtains to regulate temperature, and switching off heating when not required. We have double glazing now which helps a lot. My grandkids always tease me because we're so fussy about wasting energy! But honestly, every little bit counts, and being mindful of our consumption has helped reduce those bills. And I must say, since my husband passed away, there isn't much need for an extra heater – sometimes too warm already feels like overkill! Still, I make sure to check thermostats regularly and adjust accordingly.

To be honest, I'm still getting used to them all. My grandkids got me a wireless speaker system a while back, which is quite nice, really helps me hear what's going on better since my hearing isn't what it used to be. But other than that, I think that's the extent of my tech savvy. I mean, I know how to use the basics like turning it on and off and adjusting the volume, but I wouldn't say I fully grasp everything these gadgets have to offer. Still, I suppose it makes life easier for everyone in the household – my granddaughter loves playing music and podcasts with it!

I think prioritizing is all about weighing what's most important to me, isn't it? For instance, my grandkids come first, so spending quality time with them always takes precedence. But other priorities like keeping a tidy garden, staying fit and healthy, and managing the finances aren't far behind either. It's really about finding balance between different aspects of life. If one thing suffers, another might gain, which helps prevent burnout. My motto has been "patience and curiosity" – being mindful of how each decision affects my overall well-being and making sure nothing gets neglected.
